text: The patient is in a supine position under general anesthesia. Two 5mm incisions are made on both sides of the lower abdomen. A blunt tip cannula is used to inject 200 to 300 ml of a solution containing lidocaine and adrenaline into the subcutaneous fat of the lower abdomen.

The surgeon uses a 3.5 mm liposuction cannula and a 50 ml syringe with a lock to manually collect approximately 100 ml of Lipoaz Pirate. The collected suspension is then transferred to 10 ml syringes and centrifuged for three minutes at 1000 rpm.
Pressure plasters are applied over the patient's incisions to minimize hematoma formation. The patient is then repositioned to a lithotomy position in preparation for the injection phase.

The surgeon expels the aqueous infranatant fluid from the syringe, leaving only the fat. Using a three-way stopcock and two ten-ml syringes, the adipose tissue is homogenized and softened by repeatedly transferring it between the syringes, preparing it for injection.

The surgeon curettes the fistula tract through the external opening, removing granulation tissue. A ureteral catheter is tied to the seton. The internal opening is debrided and curetted in preparation for closure. Three pre-taken absorbable sutures are prepared, including the internal sphincter, to be tied at the end of the procedure.

The surgeon makes two small incisions at each side of the external opening of the fistula. Adipose tissue is injected around the internal opening and along the fistula tract. The injection is repeated at various levels using multiple passes from the internal to the external opening until a firm swelling surrounds the fistula tract.

The surgeon uses a sharp Coleman Cobra head fat injection cannula to cut the fistula tract transversely, releasing fibrotic scar tissue and allowing adipose tissue to be interposed between the cut ends. The three pre-taken absorbable sutures at the internal opening are then tied.
